Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

end of world model.

Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state more carefully
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("All nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    if n['pixels'] < 100 or n['color'] != 'S':
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Check adjacency
print("\nAdjacency (small nodes):")
for edge in seg['adjacency_list']:
    i, j = edge
    ni = next((n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['id'] == i), None)
    nj = next((n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['id'] == j), None)
    if ni and nj and (ni['pixels'] < 100 or nj['pixels'] < 100):
        print(f"  {i}({ni['color']}) - {j}({nj['color']})")

# Let's try rotating to see if configuration changes
result = action(['SPACE'])
print("\nAfter SPACE:", result['state'])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter SPACE nodes:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    if n['pixels'] < 100 or n['color'] != 'S':
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Count red and G blocks
r_count = sum(1 for n in seg2['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'R')
g_count = sum(1 for n in seg2['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'G')
print(f"\nR blocks: {r_count}, G blocks: {g_count}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

# Check current state more carefully
seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Count red and G blocks
